question: 7. Let \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n} \) be a permutation of the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, n \). Call \( a_{i} \) a "big" integer if \( a_{i}>a_{j} \) for all \( j>i \). Find the mean number of "big" integers over all permutations on the first \( n \) positive integers.
solution:
First Solution. If \( \sigma \) is a permutation, let \( N_{i}(\sigma) \) be the number of "big" integers occurring at position \( i \). Then \( N_{i}(\sigma)=0 \) or 1 . The average value of \( N_{i}(\sigma) \) over all the permutations is \( 1 /(n-i+1) \) because after \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots \), \( a_{i-1} \) have been selected, the question of whether or not \( a_{i} \) will be a big integer is whether or not \( a_{i} \) is the greatest among the ( \( n-i+1 \) ) integers left.

Let the number of big integers in \( \sigma \) be \( N(\sigma) \). Then \( N(\sigma)=N_{1}(\sigma)+N_{2}(\sigma) \) \( +\cdots+N_{n}(\sigma) \), and the average value of \( N(\sigma) \) over all the \( n \) ! permutations will be the sum of the average values of the separate terms, \( N_{i}(\sigma) \), for \( i= \) \( 1,2, \ldots, n \). Hence this average is
\[
1 / n+1 /(n-1)+1 /(n-2)+\cdots+1
\]

Second Solution. The average number of big integers in a permutation of \( n \) distinct integers is the same no matter what these integers are. Call this number \( A_{n} \).

Given a permutation of \( \{1,2, \ldots n\} \), remove the element 1 and close up to obtain a permutation of \( \{2,3, \ldots, n\} \). This defines an \( n \)-to- 1 mapping \( \xi \) of permutations of \( \{1,2, \ldots, n\} \) into permutations of \( \{2,3, \ldots, n\} \). If the element 1 appeared at the end of the original permutation \( \sigma \), then \( \xi(\sigma) \) has one big integer fewer than \( \sigma \). In all other cases \( \xi(\sigma) \) has the same number of big integers as \( \sigma \). If \( N(\sigma) \) denotes the number of big integers in \( \sigma \). this means that
\[
N(\sigma)=N(\xi(\sigma))+1 \quad \text { for }(n-1)!\text { permutations } \sigma
\]
and
\[
N(\sigma)=N(\xi(\sigma)) \quad \text { for the remaining permutations. }
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{n} N(\sigma)=\sum_{n} N(\xi(\sigma))+(n-1)!.
\]

Dividing through by \( n! \) we see that
\[
A_{n}=\frac{1}{n!} \sum_{\sigma} N(\xi(\sigma))+1 / n
\]

Since the mapping \( \xi \) is always exactly \( \boldsymbol{n} \)-to- 1 .
\[
\frac{1}{n!} \sum_{n} N(\xi(\sigma))=A_{n-1}
\]

This gives the recurrence
\[
A_{n}=A_{n-1}+1 / n .
\]

Since \( A_{1}=1 \). it follows that
\[
A_{n}=1+1 / 2+1 / 3+\cdots+1 / n
\]

descriptive_long.question: 7. Let \( elementone, elementtwo, \ldots, elementlast \) be a permutation of the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, totalnum \). Call \( elementi \) a "big" integer if \( elementi>elementj \) for all \( indexj>indexi \). Find the mean number of "big" integers over all permutations on the first \( totalnum \) positive integers.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. If \( permute \) is a permutation, let \( bigcounti(permute) \) be the number of "big" integers occurring at position \( indexi \). Then \( bigcounti(permute)=0 \) or 1. The average value of \( bigcounti(permute) \) over all the permutations is \( 1 /(totalnum-indexi+1) \) because after \( elementone, elementtwo, \ldots, elementi-1 \) have been selected, the question of whether or not \( elementi \) will be a big integer is whether or not \( elementi \) is the greatest among the ( \( totalnum-indexi+1 \) ) integers left.

Let the number of big integers in \( permute \) be \( bigcount(permute) \). Then \( bigcount(permute)=bigcountone(permute)+bigcounttwo(permute)+\cdots+bigcountlast(permute) \), and the average value of \( bigcount(permute) \) over all the \( totalnum ! \) permutations will be the sum of the average values of the separate terms, \( bigcounti(permute) \), for \( indexi =1,2, \ldots, totalnum \). Hence this average is
\[
1 / totalnum+1 /(totalnum-1)+1 /(totalnum-2)+\cdots+1
\]

Second Solution. The average number of big integers in a permutation of \( totalnum \) distinct integers is the same no matter what these integers are. Call this number \( avgcount_{totalnum} \).

Given a permutation of \{1,2, \ldots, totalnum\}, remove the element 1 and close up to obtain a permutation of \{2,3, \ldots, totalnum\}. This defines an \( totalnum \)-to-1 mapping \( compress \) of permutations of \{1,2, \ldots, totalnum\} into permutations of \{2,3, \ldots, totalnum\}. If the element 1 appeared at the end of the original permutation \( permute \), then \( compress(permute) \) has one big integer fewer than \( permute \). In all other cases \( compress(permute) \) has the same number of big integers as \( permute \). If \( bigcount(permute) \) denotes the number of big integers in \( permute \), this means that
\[
bigcount(permute)=bigcount(compress(permute))+1 \quad \text { for }(totalnum-1)!\text { permutations } permute
\]
and
\[
bigcount(permute)=bigcount(compress(permute)) \quad \text { for the remaining permutations. }
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{totalnum} bigcount(permute)=\sum_{totalnum} bigcount(compress(permute))+(totalnum-1)!.
\]

Dividing through by \( totalnum! \) we see that
\[
avgcount_{totalnum}=\frac{1}{totalnum!} \sum_{permute} bigcount(compress(permute))+1 / totalnum
\]

Since the mapping \( compress \) is always exactly \( \boldsymbol{totalnum} \)-to-1,
\[
\frac{1}{totalnum!} \sum_{totalnum} bigcount(compress(permute))=avgcountprev
\]

This gives the recurrence
\[
avgcount_{totalnum}=avgcountprev+1 / totalnum .
\]

Since \( avgcountone=1 \), it follows that
\[
avgcount_{totalnum}=1+1 / 2+1 / 3+\cdots+1 / totalnum
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 7. Let \( raspberries, blueberries, \ldots, strawberries \) be a permutation of the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, crocodile \). Call \( butterscotch \) a "big" integer if \( butterscotch>gingerbread \) for all \( marigold>hydrangea \). Find the mean number of "big" integers over all permutations on the first \( crocodile \) positive integers.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. If \( hummingbird \) is a permutation, let \( chandelier(hummingbird) \) be the number of "big" integers occurring at position \( hydrangea \). Then \( chandelier(hummingbird)=0 \) or 1 . The average value of \( chandelier(hummingbird) \) over all the permutations is \( 1 /(crocodile-hydrangea+1) \) because after \( raspberries, blueberries, \ldots, \), \( a_{i-1} \) have been selected, the question of whether or not \( butterscotch \) will be a big integer is whether or not \( butterscotch \) is the greatest among the ( \( crocodile-hydrangea+1 \) ) integers left.

Let the number of big integers in \( hummingbird \) be \( quarterback(hummingbird) \). Then \( quarterback(hummingbird)=dragonfruit(hummingbird)+watercress(hummingbird) +\cdots+butternut(hummingbird) \), and the average value of \( quarterback(hummingbird) \) over all the \( crocodile \) ! permutations will be the sum of the average values of the separate terms, \( chandelier(hummingbird) \), for \( hydrangea= \) \( 1,2, \ldots, crocodile \). Hence this average is
\[
1 / crocodile+1 /(crocodile-1)+1 /(crocodile-2)+\cdots+1
\]

Second Solution. The average number of big integers in a permutation of \( crocodile \) distinct integers is the same no matter what these integers are. Call this number \( windbreaker \).

Given a permutation of \( \{1,2, \ldots crocodile\} \), remove the element 1 and close up to obtain a permutation of \( \{2,3, \ldots, crocodile\} \). This defines an \( crocodile \)-to- 1 mapping \( alligator \) of permutations of \( \{1,2, \ldots, crocodile\} \) into permutations of \( \{2,3, \ldots, crocodile\} \). If the element 1 appeared at the end of the original permutation \( hummingbird \), then \( alligator(hummingbird) \) has one big integer fewer than \( hummingbird \). In all other cases \( alligator(hummingbird) \) has the same number of big integers as \( hummingbird \). If \( quarterback(hummingbird) \) denotes the number of big integers in \( hummingbird \). this means that
\[
quarterback(hummingbird)=quarterback(alligator(hummingbird))+1 \quad \text { for }(crocodile-1)!\text { permutations } hummingbird
\]
and
\[
quarterback(hummingbird)=quarterback(alligator(hummingbird)) \quad \text { for the remaining permutations. }
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{crocodile} quarterback(hummingbird)=\sum_{crocodile} quarterback(alligator(hummingbird))+(crocodile-1)!.
\]

Dividing through by \( crocodile! \) we see that
\[
windbreaker=\frac{1}{crocodile!} \sum_{hummingbird} quarterback(alligator(hummingbird))+1 / crocodile
\]

Since the mapping \( alligator \) is always exactly \( \boldsymbol{crocodile} \)-to- 1 .
\[
\frac{1}{crocodile!} \sum_{crocodile} quarterback(alligator(hummingbird))=snowblower
\]

This gives the recurrence
\[
windbreaker=snowblower+1 / crocodile .
\]

Since \( matchstick=1 \). it follows that
\[
windbreaker=1+1 / 2+1 / 3+\cdots+1 / crocodile
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 7. Let \( lastentry, nextlast, \ldots, beginning \) be a permutation of the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, fixedsize \). Call \( randomnum \) a "big" integer if \( randomnum>differnum \) for all \( alltotal>aggregate \). Find the mean number of "big" integers over all permutations on the first \( fixedsize \) positive integers.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. If \( steadiness \) is a permutation, let \( tinycount(steadiness) \) be the number of "big" integers occurring at position \( aggregate \). Then \( tinycount(steadiness)=0 \) or 1. The average value of \( tinycount(steadiness) \) over all the permutations is \( 1 /(fixedsize-aggregate+1) \) because after \( lastentry, nextlast, \ldots, a_{aggregate-1} \) have been selected, the question of whether or not \( randomnum \) will be a big integer is whether or not \( randomnum \) is the greatest among the ( \( fixedsize-aggregate+1 \) ) integers left.

Let the number of big integers in \( steadiness \) be \( smallcount(steadiness) \). Then \( smallcount(steadiness)=tinycount(steadiness)+tinycount(steadiness) +\cdots+tinycount(steadiness) \), and the average value of \( smallcount(steadiness) \) over all the \( fixedsize! \) permutations will be the sum of the average values of the separate terms, \( tinycount(steadiness) \), for \( aggregate=1,2, \ldots, fixedsize \). Hence this average is
\[
1 / fixedsize+1 /(fixedsize-1)+1 /(fixedsize-2)+\cdots+1
\]

Second Solution. The average number of big integers in a permutation of \( fixedsize \) distinct integers is the same no matter what these integers are. Call this number \( totalsumn \).

Given a permutation of \{1,2, \ldots fixedsize\}, remove the element 1 and close up to obtain a permutation of \{2,3, \ldots, fixedsize\}. This defines an \( fixedsize \)-to-1 mapping \( stability \) of permutations of \{1,2, \ldots, fixedsize\} into permutations of \{2,3, \ldots, fixedsize\}. If the element 1 appeared at the end of the original permutation \( steadiness \), then \( stability(steadiness) \) has one big integer fewer than \( steadiness \). In all other cases \( stability(steadiness) \) has the same number of big integers as \( steadiness \). If \( smallcount(steadiness) \) denotes the number of big integers in \( steadiness \) this means that
\[
smallcount(steadiness)=smallcount(stability(steadiness))+1 \quad \text { for }(fixedsize-1)!\text { permutations } steadiness
\]
and
\[
smallcount(steadiness)=smallcount(stability(steadiness)) \quad \text { for the remaining permutations. }
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{fixedsize} smallcount(steadiness)=\sum_{fixedsize} smallcount(stability(steadiness))+(fixedsize-1)!.
\]

Dividing through by \( fixedsize! \) we see that
\[
 totalsumn=\frac{1}{fixedsize!} \sum_{steadiness} smallcount(stability(steadiness))+1 / fixedsize
\]

Since the mapping \( stability \) is always exactly \( \boldsymbol{fixedsize} \)-to-1 .
\[
\frac{1}{fixedsize!} \sum_{fixedsize} smallcount(stability(steadiness))=totalsumprevious
\]

This gives the recurrence
\[
 totalsumn=totalsumprevious+1 / fixedsize .
\]

Since \( totalsumone=1 \). it follows that
\[
 totalsumn=1+1 / 2+1 / 3+\cdots+1 / fixedsize
\]

garbled_string.question: 7. Let \( sordkfynm, vclimgepr, \ldots, kwjzfehio \) be a permutation of the integers \( 1,2, \ldots, pjroxdiae \). Call \( xiejahtru \) a "big" integer if \( xiejahtru>nquoptyel \) for all \( hjgrksla>qzxwvtnp \). Find the mean number of "big" integers over all permutations on the first \( pjroxdiae \) positive integers.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. If \( bvlacmker \) is a permutation, let \( wamnzcbrt(bvlacmker) \) be the number of "big" integers occurring at position \( qzxwvtnp \). Then \( wamnzcbrt(bvlacmker)=0 \) or 1. The average value of \( wamnzcbrt(bvlacmker) \) over all the permutations is \( 1 /(pjroxdiae-qzxwvtnp+1) \) because after \( sordkfynm, vclimgepr, \ldots, a_{qzxwvtnp-1} \) have been selected, the question of whether or not \( xiejahtru \) will be a big integer is whether or not \( xiejahtru \) is the greatest among the ( \( pjroxdiae-qzxwvtnp+1 \) ) integers left.

Let the number of big integers in \( bvlacmker \) be \( tdufhplok(bvlacmker) \). Then \( tdufhplok(bvlacmker)=gepluvhqa(bvlacmker)+mobqtrzen(bvlacmker)+\cdots+yfsckdlar(bvlacmker) \), and the average value of \( tdufhplok(bvlacmker) \) over all the \( pjroxdiae \)! permutations will be the sum of the average values of the separate terms, \( wamnzcbrt(bvlacmker) \), for \( qzxwvtnp=1,2, \ldots, pjroxdiae \). Hence this average is
\[
1 / pjroxdiae+1 /(pjroxdiae-1)+1 /(pjroxdiae-2)+\cdots+1
\]

Second Solution. The average number of big integers in a permutation of \( pjroxdiae \) distinct integers is the same no matter what these integers are. Call this number \( zufqlenmx \).

Given a permutation of \( \{1,2, \ldots pjroxdiae\} \), remove the element 1 and close up to obtain a permutation of \( \{2,3, \ldots, pjroxdiae\} \). This defines an \( pjroxdiae \)-to-1 mapping \( ruqsonpey \) of permutations of \{1,2, \ldots, pjroxdiae\} into permutations of \{2,3, \ldots, pjroxdiae\}. If the element 1 appeared at the end of the original permutation \( bvlacmker \), then \( ruqsonpey(bvlacmker) \) has one big integer fewer than \( bvlacmker \). In all other cases \( ruqsonpey(bvlacmker) \) has the same number of big integers as \( bvlacmker \). If \( tdufhplok(bvlacmker) \) denotes the number of big integers in \( bvlacmker \), this means that
\[
tdufhplok(bvlacmker)=tdufhplok(ruqsonpey(bvlacmker))+1 \quad \text { for }(pjroxdiae-1)!\text { permutations } bvlacmker
\]
and
\[
tdufhplok(bvlacmker)=tdufhplok(ruqsonpey(bvlacmker)) \quad \text { for the remaining permutations. }
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{pjroxdiae} tdufhplok(bvlacmker)=\sum_{pjroxdiae} tdufhplok(ruqsonpey(bvlacmker))+(pjroxdiae-1)!.
\]

Dividing through by \( pjroxdiae! \) we see that
\[
zufqlenmx=\frac{1}{pjroxdiae!} \sum_{bvlacmker} tdufhplok(ruqsonpey(bvlacmker))+1 / pjroxdiae
\]

Since the mapping \( ruqsonpey \) is always exactly \( \mathbf{pjroxdiae} \)-to-1,
\[
\frac{1}{pjroxdiae!} \sum_{pjroxdiae} tdufhplok(ruqsonpey(bvlacmker))=vkptaejro
\]

This gives the recurrence
\[
zufqlenmx=vkptaejro+1 / pjroxdiae .
\]

Since \( dhpqxirsm=1 \), it follows that
\[
zufqlenmx=1+1 / 2+1 / 3+\cdots+1 / pjroxdiae
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 2$ be fixed.  On the $d$-dimensional cubic lattice  
\[
[n]^{d}:=\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d}):1\le i_{k}\le n\;(k=1,\dots ,d)\Bigr\}
\]
place the $N:=n^{d}$ distinct ``masses'' $1,2,\dots ,N$ by means of a
uniformly random bijection  
\[
m:[n]^{d}\longrightarrow\{1,2,\dots ,N\}.
\]

For $v=(v_{1},\dots ,v_{d})$ put  
\[
Q(v):=\Bigl\{w\in[n]^{d}:w_{k}\ge v_{k}\;(1\le k\le d)\Bigr\},
\qquad
s(v):=|Q(v)|=\prod_{k=1}^{d}(n-v_{k}+1).
\]

A vertex $v$ is said to be \emph{deep-light} if  
\[
m(v)<m(w)\qquad(\forall w\in Q(v)\setminus\{v\}).
\]

Define  
\[
X_{n,d}:=\#\Bigl\{v\in[n]^{d}:v\text{ is deep--light}\Bigr\},
\qquad
H_{n}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{k}.
\]

1.  Prove that  
\[
\mathbb{E}[X_{n,d}]=(H_{n})^{d}.
\]

2.  Show that there exists a constant $C_{d}<\infty$, depending only on
$d$, such that  
\[
\operatorname{Var}\!\bigl(X_{n,d}\bigr)\le C_{d}\,(H_{n})^{\,2d}. \tag{$\dagger$}
\]

3.  Deduce from $(\dagger)$ that the sequence  
\[
Y_{n,d}:=\frac{X_{n,d}}{(H_{n})^{\,d}}
\]
is \emph{uniformly $L^{2}$-bounded}.  Conclude, in particular, that the
family $\{Y_{n,d}:n\ge 1\}$ is tight.

(The standard estimates $H_{n}=\log n+\gamma+O(n^{-1})$ and
$\displaystyle\sum_{k>t}k^{-2}\le t^{-1}$ may be used without proof.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
N:=n^{d},\qquad
I_{v}:=\mathbf 1_{\{v\text{ is deep--light}\}},\qquad
X_{n,d}=\sum_{v\in[n]^{d}} I_{v}.
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.\;Expectation
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Fix $v\in[n]^{d}$.  Inside $Q(v)$ the restriction
$m|_{Q(v)}$ is a uniformly random permutation of the $s(v)$ masses
located there.  Every one of the $s(v)$ sites therefore
receives the smallest mass in $Q(v)$ with probability $1/s(v)$, whence  
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_{v}=1)=\frac{1}{s(v)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathbb{E}[X_{n,d}]
=\sum_{v\in[n]^{d}}\frac{1}{s(v)}
      =\biggl(\sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{1}{t}\biggr)^{d}
      =(H_{n})^{d}.
\]
\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
2.\;Variance upper bound
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Write  
\[
A:=Q(u),\quad a:=|A|=s(u),\qquad
B:=Q(v),\quad b:=|B|=s(v).
\]
Set $S:=A\cup B$ and $s:=|S|$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2A.\;A universal covariance bound
----------------------------------------------------------------
Fix \emph{distinct} vertices $u,v$ and assume without loss of
generality that $a\le b$.
We establish
\[
\bigl|\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\bigr|
      \le\frac{C}{ab}\qquad(\text{with }C=3). \tag{2.1}
\]

\emph{Proof of (2.1).}
Because
$\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})
      =\mathbb{P}(I_{u}=I_{v}=1)-\dfrac{1}{ab}$,
it suffices to show
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_{u}=I_{v}=1)\le\frac{2}{ab}. \tag{2.2}
\]

Reveal first the relative order of the $b$ masses inside $B=Q(v)$.
Among the $b!$ equiprobable orders, $v$ carries the smallest mass in
exactly $(b-1)!$ of them, hence
\[
\mathbb{P}\bigl(I_{v}=1\bigr)=\frac{1}{b}.
\]

Condition on the event $I_{v}=1$.
Two cases occur.

(i) $u\notin B$.  
Then \emph{no} information has been revealed about the ordering of the
masses inside $A=Q(u)$, whence
\[
\mathbb{P}\bigl(I_{u}=1\,\bigm|\,I_{v}=1\bigr)=\frac{1}{a}.
\]

(ii) $u\in B$.  
Since $v$ is now known to be the smallest in $B$, the conditional
distribution of the remaining $b-1$ masses in $B\setminus\{v\}$ is
still uniform.  Inside $A=Q(u)$ the vertex $v$ is \emph{not} present,
so $u$ must beat the remaining $a-1$ points of $A\setminus\{u\}$.
Consequently
\[
\mathbb{P}\bigl(I_{u}=1\,\bigm|\,I_{v}=1\bigr)
      \le\frac{1}{a-1}\le\frac{2}{a},
      \qquad\text{since }a\ge2.
\]

In both cases
$\mathbb{P}(I_{u}=1\,\&\,I_{v}=1)\le\dfrac{2}{ab}$, proving (2.2).
Combining (2.2) with $\mathbb{P}(I_{u}=1)\mathbb{P}(I_{v}=1)=1/(ab)$
yields (2.1).
\hfill$\square$

----------------------------------------------------------------
2B.\;Arithmetic preliminaries
----------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce
\[
\Sigma_{d}(t):=\#\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})\in\mathbb{N}^{d}:
                      i_{1}\cdots i_{d}\le t\Bigr\},
\qquad
\Delta_{d}(t):=\Sigma_{d}(t)-\Sigma_{d}(t-1)
              =\#\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d}):
                      i_{1}\cdots i_{d}=t\Bigr\}.
\]

A classic divisor-counting estimate (cf.\ Hardy-Wright, Chap.\;V)
implies
\[
\Sigma_{d}(t)\le C_{d}\,t(\log t)^{d-1}\quad(t\ge2), \tag{2.3}
\]
hence
\[
\Delta_{d}(t)\le C_{d}\,(\log t)^{\,d-1}\quad(t\ge3). \tag{2.4}
\]

Moreover, for every $\alpha>0$ and $M\ge3$,
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{M}\frac{(\log t)^{\alpha}}{t}
  \le C_{\alpha}\,(\log M)^{\alpha+1}. \tag{2.5}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
2C.\;Bounding the double sum
----------------------------------------------------------------
Decompose
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})
=\sum_{v}\operatorname{Var}(I_{v})
+2\sum_{u<v}\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v}).  \tag{2.6}
\]

\emph{Diagonal part.}  Since $0\le I_{v}\le1$,
\[
\sum_{v}\operatorname{Var}(I_{v})
       \le\sum_{v}\mathbb{E}[I_{v}]
       =(H_{n})^{d}.                                     \tag{2.7}
\]

\emph{Mixed part.}  Order the pair $(u,v)$ so that $a=s(u)\le b=s(v)$.
From (2.1),
\[
\bigl|\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\bigr|
      \le\frac{3}{ab}=\frac{3}{b}\cdot\frac{1}{a}.       \tag{2.8}
\]

Fix $v$ and set
\[
W_{v}:=\sum_{\substack{u\neq v\\ a\le b}}\frac{1}{a}
      =\sum_{u\neq v}\mathbf 1_{\{s(u)\le s(v)\}}\frac{1}{s(u)}.
\]
Replacing $i_{k}:=n-u_{k}+1$ provides a bijection from the set of $u$
counted in $W_{v}$ to  
\[
\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})\in\mathbb{N}^{d}:
      i_{1}\cdots i_{d}\le b\Bigr\},
\]
so that, using (2.3) and (2.5) with $\alpha=d-1$,
\[
W_{v}\le\sum_{t=1}^{b}\frac{\Delta_{d}(t)}{t}
      \le C_{d}\sum_{t=3}^{b}\frac{(\log t)^{d-1}}{t}
      \le C_{d}\,(\log b)^{d}.                           \tag{2.9}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{u<v}\bigl|\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\bigr|
 \le 3\sum_{v}\frac{W_{v}}{b}
 \le 3C_{d}\sum_{v}\frac{(\log s(v))^{d}}{s(v)}.         \tag{2.10}
\]

Group vertices according to the value $t=s(v)$ and apply (2.4):
\[
\sum_{v}\frac{(\log s(v))^{d}}{s(v)}
  =\sum_{t=1}^{n^{d}}\frac{(\log t)^{d}}{t}\,\Delta_{d}(t)
  \le C_{d}\sum_{t=3}^{n^{d}}\frac{(\log t)^{2d-1}}{t}.  \tag{2.11}
\]

Finally, (2.5) with $\alpha=2d-1$ gives
\[
\sum_{t=3}^{n^{d}}\frac{(\log t)^{2d-1}}{t}
\le C_{d}\,(\log n^{d})^{2d}
 = C_{d}\,(d\log n)^{2d}
 \le C_{d}\,(H_{n})^{2d}.                              \tag{2.12}
\]

Combining (2.6)-(2.12),
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})
 \le (H_{n})^{d}+C_{d}'(H_{n})^{2d}
 \le C_{d}''(H_{n})^{2d}\qquad(n\ge2),
\]
establishing $(\dagger)$.
\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
3.\;Uniform $L^{2}$-boundedness
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Since $\mathbb{E}[Y_{n,d}]=1$ and by $(\dagger)$
\[
\operatorname{Var}(Y_{n,d})
      =\frac{\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})}{(H_{n})^{2d}}
      \le\frac{C_{d}}{1}=C_{d},
\]
the family $\{Y_{n,d}\}_{n\ge1}$ is bounded in $L^{2}$ and therefore
tight.
\hfill$\square$

----------------------------------------------------------------
References  

[1] G.\;H. Hardy and E.\;M. Wright, \emph{An Introduction to the Theory
of Numbers}, 6th ed., Oxford Univ.\;Press, 2008.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension:  the problem generalises the 1-dimensional
   “record’’ concept to an arbitrary fixed number d ≥ 2 of dimensions,
   increasing the state space from n to nᵈ sites.

2. Additional constraints:  a site must be lighter than every crate in
   a d-dimensional positive orthant, a much larger and overlapping set
   of competitors than in the 1-dimensional case.

3. Deeper theory:  
   – Multi–index harmonic sums appear; separating variables is
     essential to compute the expectation.  
   – To bound the variance one must control covariances across a highly
     inter-dependent family of indicator variables, requiring a careful
     classification into comparable and incomparable pairs and an
     estimate that hinges on geometric properties of the lattice.  
   – Probabilistic limit results (Chebyshev together with (∗)) are
     needed to prove convergence in probability.

4. Multiple interacting concepts:  the solution blends enumerative
   combinatorics (orthant sizes), multi–dimensional harmonic–analysis,
   dependence estimates, and probabilistic limit theory.

5. Greater length and depth:  compared with the single–sum harmonic
   computation of the original problem, the enhanced variant demands
   a multi–sum analysis, careful covariance bounds, and an explicit
   asymptotic argument, each of which is several technical steps
   longer than the entire original solution.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 2$ be fixed.  
On the $d$-dimensional cubic lattice  
\[
[n]^{d}:=\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d}) : 1\le i_{k}\le n\;(k=1,\dots ,d)\Bigr\}
\]
place the $N:=n^{d}$ distinct ``masses'' $1,2,\dots ,N$ by a uniformly
random bijection  
\[
m:[n]^{d}\longrightarrow\{1,2,\dots ,N\}.
\]

For $v=(v_{1},\dots ,v_{d})$ put  
\[
Q(v):=\Bigl\{w\in[n]^{d}: w_{k}\ge v_{k}\;(1\le k\le d)\Bigr\},
\qquad
s(v):=|Q(v)|=\prod_{k=1}^{d}(n-v_{k}+1).
\]

A vertex $v$ is called \emph{deep-light} if  
\[
m(v)<m(w)\qquad(\forall w\in Q(v)\setminus\{v\}).
\]

Write  
\[
X_{n,d}:=\#\Bigl\{v\in[n]^{d}:v\text{ is deep--light}\Bigr\},
\qquad
H_{n}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{k}.
\]

1.  Prove that  
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[X_{n,d}\bigr]=\bigl(H_{n}\bigr)^{d}.
\]

2.  Show that there is a finite constant $C_{d}$, depending only on
$d$, such that  
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})\le C_{d}\,\bigl(H_{n}\bigr)^{\,2d-1}. \tag{$\dagger$}
\]

3.  Deduce from $(\dagger)$ that  
\[
\frac{X_{n,d}}{\bigl(H_{n}\bigr)^{\,d}}
          \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{L^{2}}1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac{X_{n,d}}{\bigl(H_{n}\bigr)^{\,d}}
          \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{\mathbb{P}}1 .
\]

(The estimates $H_{n}=\log n+\gamma+O(n^{-1})$ and
$\displaystyle\sum_{k>t}k^{-2}\le t^{-1}$ may be used without proof.)

-----------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
N:=n^{d},\qquad
I_{v}:=\mathbf 1_{\{v\text{ is deep--light}\}},\qquad
X_{n,d}=\sum_{v\in[n]^{d}} I_{v},
\quad\text{and}\quad
u<v\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;u\neq v\text{ and the ordered pair is taken once. }
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
1.  The expectation  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  

For a fixed vertex $v$ the restriction of $m$ to
the orthant $Q(v)$ is a uniformly random permutation of the
$s(v)$ masses located there.  Each site in $Q(v)$ therefore
receives the lightest of those $s(v)$ masses with probability
$1/s(v)$, whence  
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_{v}=1)=\frac{1}{s(v)}.
\]
Consequently  
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[X_{n,d}\bigr]
      =\sum_{v\in[n]^{d}}\frac{1}{s(v)}
      =\Bigl(\sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{1}{t}\Bigr)^{d}
      =(H_{n})^{d}.
\]
\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
2.  An upper bound for $\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})$  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  

We first establish a universal bound for  
$\lvert\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\rvert$ and then show that the
resulting double sum is  
$O\!\bigl((H_{n})^{2d-1}\bigr)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------  
2A.  A precise covariance bound  
----------------------------------------------------------------  

Fix two distinct vertices $u\neq v$ and put  
\[
A:=Q(u),\qquad B:=Q(v),\qquad
a:=|A|,\quad b:=|B|.
\]

(i)  \emph{Nested orthants ($A\subset B$ or $B\subset A$).}  
Assume, for definiteness, $A\subset B$.  
Because $v\notin A$ (the strict inclusion forces $v$ to lie outside
$A$ while $u\in B$), the events  
\[
I_{v}=1\; \Longleftrightarrow\; m(v)=\min_{B}m,\qquad
I_{u}=1\; \Longleftrightarrow\; m(u)=\min_{A}m
\]
are \emph{independent}.  
Indeed, after conditioning on $I_{v}=1$ the smallest mass in $B$
is fixed at $v$ and the remaining $b-1$ masses are still arranged
uniformly at random, so the conditional probability that $u$ is the
minimum in $A$ is exactly $1/a$.  Thus  
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_{u}=I_{v}=1)=\frac{1}{a\,b}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})=0.
\]

(ii)  \emph{Non-nested orthants ($A\not\subset B$ and $B\not\subset
A$).}  
Write
\[
s:=|A\cup B|=a+b-r,\qquad r:=|A\cap B|.
\]
Because $u\notin B$ and $v\notin A$, the two events $I_{u}=1$ and
$I_{v}=1$ require that the two smallest masses inside $A\cup B$ fall on
$u$ and $v$ (in either order).  All $s$ positions are equally likely,
so  
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_{u}=I_{v}=1)=\frac{2}{s(s-1)}
                       \le\frac{2}{\max\{a,b\}^{2}}.
\]

Combining (i)-(ii) we obtain the \emph{uniform} bound  
\[
\lvert\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\rvert
      \le\frac{2}{\max\{a,b\}^{2}}
      \qquad(u\neq v). \tag{2.1}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------  
2B.  A summation lemma  
----------------------------------------------------------------  

For $t\ge 1$ put  
\[
\Sigma_{d}(t):=\#\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})\in\mathbb{N}^{d}:
                      i_{1}\cdots i_{d}\le t\Bigr\}.
\]
A standard divisor-counting argument (see
Hardy-Wright, Chap.~V) yields  
\[
\Sigma_{d}(t)\le C_{d}\,t\,\bigl(\log t\bigr)^{d-1}, \qquad t\ge 2, \tag{2.2}
\]
for some constant $C_{d}$ depending only on $d$.  By partial
summation,
\[
\sum_{s\le t}s
      \le C_{d}\,t^{2}\,(\log t)^{d-1},               \tag{2.3}
\qquad
\sum_{s\le t}\frac{1}{s}
      \le C_{d}\,(\log t)^{d}.                        \tag{2.4}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------  
2C.  Bounding the double sum of covariances  
----------------------------------------------------------------  

Write the variance as
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})
   =\sum_{v}\operatorname{Var}(I_{v})
    +2\sum_{u<v}\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v}). \tag{2.5}
\]

The diagonal contribution satisfies
\[
\sum_{v}\operatorname{Var}(I_{v})
       \le\sum_{v}\mathbb{E}[I_{v}]
       =(H_{n})^{d}.                                   \tag{2.6}
\]

Turn to the mixed terms.  Order the vertices so that
$s(u)\le s(v)$.  From (2.1) we get  
\[
\bigl|\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\bigr|
      \le\frac{2}{s(v)^{2}}\qquad(u\neq v).            \tag{2.7}
\]
Hence  
\[
\sum_{u<v}\lvert\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\rvert
 \le 2\sum_{v}\frac{1}{s(v)^{2}}
       \sum_{\substack{u:u\neq v\\ s(u)\le s(v)}}\!s(u). \tag{2.8}
\]

For the inner sum we invoke (2.3) with $t=s(v)$:
\[
\sum_{\substack{u:u\neq v\\ s(u)\le s(v)}}\!s(u)
       \le C_{d}\,s(v)\,\bigl(\log s(v)\bigr)^{d-1}.   \tag{2.9}
\]

Combine (2.8)-(2.9) and use (2.4):
\[
\sum_{u<v}\lvert\operatorname{Cov}(I_{u},I_{v})\rvert
 \le 2C_{d}\sum_{v}\frac{(\log s(v))^{d-1}}{s(v)}
 \le 2C_{d}\bigl(H_{n}\bigr)^{2d-1}.                   \tag{2.10}
\]

Finally, gather (2.6) and (2.10):
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})
      \le (H_{n})^{d}+2C_{d}(H_{n})^{2d-1}
      \le C_{d}'(H_{n})^{2d-1}\qquad(n\ge 2),         \tag{2.11}
\]
where $C_{d}'=1+2C_{d}$.  This proves $(\dagger)$.
\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
3.  $L^{2}$-law of large numbers  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  

By Parts 1-2,  
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\Bigl[\bigl(X_{n,d}-(H_{n})^{d}\bigr)^{2}\Bigr]
      =\operatorname{Var}(X_{n,d})
      \le C_{d}(H_{n})^{2d-1},
\]
and therefore  
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\Bigl[\Bigl(\tfrac{X_{n,d}}{(H_{n})^{d}}-1\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]
      \le\frac{C_{d}}{H_{n}}
      \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0,
\]
because $H_{n}\sim\log n\to\infty$.  Convergence in $L^{2}$ implies
convergence in probability, completing Part 3.
\hfill$\square$

-----------------------------------------------------------------  
References  

[1] G. H. Hardy and E. M. Wright, \emph{An Introduction to the Theory of
Numbers}, 6th ed., Oxford Univ. Press, 2008.  

-----------------------------------------------------------------
